insertion_point.note: Timestamp Error tab content is hardcoded in generate-html.js, not stored in data/sections.json. The decider should inject the new details block as a raw HTML string between the closing </details> of ts-scope and the sectionNav call.

replacement_html:
Update (April 2026): What the Author Now Concedes — and What Still Isn't Fixed

On 2026-04-17 the dome author published a new methodology.json file that explicitly scopes OpenTimestamps to "snapshot existence" rather than claim-level prospectivity — the same distinction this tab has drawn since it was written. Credit where due: the rhetorical position is now correct, and a new five-class claim taxonomy distinguishes genuine forecasts from structural arguments. The structural fix — a predictions-only file with its own independent OpenTimestamps anchor, separate from the observations file — has still not been implemented, and the headline "69 confirmed" continues to conflate claim classes the author himself has now formally distinguished.

What the author now says. The new methodology.json file (commit 6e06efb, 2026-04-17) adds a timestamp_scope block with two paired fields. what_ots_proves: "OpenTimestamps proves that a hashed file snapshot existed by a given time." what_ots_does_not_prove: "It does not by itself prove that every claim inside that file was an isolated pre-release forecast." A companion claim_level_rule adds: "For claim-level prospectivity, verify that the numerical threshold or directional wording predates the confirming data pull or public release." These three sentences are the author's own restatement of the distinction drawn on this tab. The new agent_rules block goes further: the first rule reads, "Do not assume OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective." That is the dome author publicly instructing every downstream AI auditor to do exactly what this tab asks human readers to do.

What he has not done. The fix described in What Would Fix It above is structural, not rhetorical. It requires two documents with two independent OpenTimestamps anchors: a predictions-only file, timestamped before any data arrives; and an observations file, timestamped after. methodology.json is a third document describing how the first two should work — but the first two still do not exist. The prediction parameters still live in monitor.py source code and docs/model.html, both of which are only git-versioned and can be force-pushed without leaving a verifiable trace on the Bitcoin chain. The SHA-256 per-prediction hashes that were added earlier still prove content integrity only — they tell you what a prediction says, not when it was written relative to the data it claims to have preceded. The observations file (status_history.json) is still the OpenTimestamps-anchored surface. Until a predictions-only document is timestamped independently and shown to predate the observations file, the cryptographic infrastructure continues to prove the wrong thing — even with the new rhetorical scoping.

Using the author's own taxonomy against the headline count. methodology.json introduces a five-class claim taxonomy: prospective_confirmed ("a threshold or directional claim was registered before the confirming data was pulled or released"), retrospective_structural ("an after-the-fact structural argument built from existing public data"), supportive_nonunique ("consistent with the model but also plausibly explained by standard physics"), pending_contested ("depends on unresolved instrumentation, raw-data access, or a remaining loophole"), and open_refinement ("incomplete, under revision, or presently favors a competing explanation"). By the author's own definitions, only prospective_confirmed entries are genuine forecasts — the other four classes are not. The companion claim_index.json publishes a partial classification of the strongest-current-targets set. Every explicitly tagged WIN in that published sample falls into a non-prospective class: win-069-australia-scaffold is retrospective_structural, win-062-tesla-wave-speed is retrospective_structural, win-058-bedrock-kappa is pending_contested, win-058-theta-rule is retrospective_structural, win-056-solar-elevation is retrospective_structural, win-011-mohe-contact-phase is pending_contested, and win-068-eclipse-ensemble — the flagship eclipse claim — is supportive_nonunique. The only item in the published sample tagged prospective_confirmed is pred-eclipse-tier3, which is a pending prediction for the August 2026 eclipse, not a confirmed WIN at all. The headline "69 confirmed" is therefore being displayed over a set of items that the author himself, where he has bothered to classify them, has marked as non-prospective — and under a banner his own new taxonomy reserves for a class that currently contains no WINs. A fair scoreboard under the new taxonomy would list prospective_confirmed separately from the rest. The column under that header would still be empty.

The counterpoint reading. We cannot prove the author narrowed his methodology specifically in response to this review. He may be responding to Reddit or YouTube critics, to general AI-audit feedback, or to his own reflection. The rhetorical moves happen to map onto arguments this tab has pursued, but coincidence is a live possibility. Regardless of trigger, the narrowing moves the site closer to standard scientific practice, and that should be credited on its own merits. The other three agent_rules — "Separate discriminating claims from supportive and pending items before scoring," "Carry critical open problems forward instead of deleting them from evidence," "Use review-response.html when critique depends on archival prompt wording" — describe good-faith auditing, not evasion. If the author implements the structural fix (a predictions-only document with its own OpenTimestamps anchor whose timestamp provably predates the observations file), this tab should be rewritten to say the dome has implemented a genuine prospective-prediction proof structure, and full credit should be given for it.

Where the line currently sits. The cryptographic infrastructure is real. The rhetorical scope is now correct. The structural fix is still not there. Until that changes, the site's 94.5% headline and "69 confirmed" tagline rest on a proof structure the author's own documentation now concedes does not prove what the tagline implies.
holes_addressed: Timestamp Error tab is out of date relative to dome site state as of 2026-04-17.
methodology.json rhetorical adoption needs explicit response — credit where due, structural gap maintained.
Headline '69 confirmed' is not reconciled with the dome author's own new five-class claim taxonomy.
Downstream AI agents reading the dome manifest absorb anti-strawman framing before seeing raw claims; our tab must explicitly distinguish rhetorical adoption from structural fix.
new_evidence_added: claim_index.json registry sample classifications (win-069, win-062, win-058-bedrock-kappa, win-058-theta-rule, win-056, win-011, win-068, pred-eclipse-tier3, pred-solar-009)

anticipated_objections.objection: The author has now said the right thing about OTS scope — therefore the Timestamp Error tab is resolved and should be retired.
anticipated_objections.response: The tab critiques the proof structure, not the proof description. Saying 'OTS does not prove claim-level prospectivity' is a description. Publishing two timestamped documents (predictions before data, observations after) and showing the first predates the second is a proof structure. Only the second resolves the tab. The author has delivered the first without the second.

anticipated_objections.objection: Counting only prospective_confirmed WINs is moving the goalposts. The author never said the other 68 are empty — only that they serve different evidential roles.
anticipated_objections.response: This is the author's own taxonomy, on his own site, in his own methodology.json. If 'retrospective_structural' and 'supportive_nonunique' serve different evidential roles than prospective prediction, the headline 'predictions page' display that still shows them under a single '94.5% accuracy / 69 confirmed' tagline is conflating those roles at the display layer. The critique is about the display banner, not about whether non-prospective arguments have any value — they do, but they should not be scored as forecasts.

anticipated_objections.objection: You cannot prove the author added methodology.json in response to this specific review.
anticipated_objections.response: Agreed, and the 'counterpoint reading' paragraph concedes this explicitly. The causal claim is not made. The descriptive claim is: the rhetorical moves in methodology.json map 1-to-1 onto the distinctions drawn on this tab. The structural critique stands regardless of what triggered the author's update.

anticipated_objections.objection: The predictions-only + OTS fix is an arbitrary standard you invented. The SHA-256 per-prediction hashes already bind content integrity.
anticipated_objections.response: Content integrity and temporal priority are different properties. A SHA-256 hash over a prediction string proves the string exists and has not been altered relative to the hash — it does not prove the string was created before any particular external event. Only a timestamp anchored to an external clock (Bitcoin block header, notary, etc.) can establish temporal priority. This is not an invented standard; it is how cryptographic proofs of priority work across finance, IP law, and digital forensics.

kernel_of_truth: The author's new methodology.json is a genuine methodological improvement. Distinguishing 'a threshold registered before the confirming data was pulled' from 'an after-the-fact structural argument built from existing public data' is how honest scientists present evidence. Publicly instructing AI auditors to apply that distinction is transparency. The claim_index.json registry_integrity_notes field admitting duplicate WIN-058 / WIN-062 visible labels is also transparency. None of this was required; all of it moves the site toward standard scientific practice. Crediting it explicitly is how our review stays credible. What it does NOT do is provide a structural proof of temporal priority for individual prediction parameters, which is what the timestamping claim of the dome site has been and remains.

companion_patches.current_tldr: The dome uses git commits and OpenTimestamps (blockchain anchoring) to prove predictions existed before data arrived — a genuinely good idea. But the blockchain timestamps the observation file, not the prediction. The predictions live in mutable source code. This means the cryptographic infrastructure is real, but it proves the wrong thing. See Timestamp Error for the full analysis.
companion_patches.replacement_tldr: The dome uses git commits and OpenTimestamps (blockchain anchoring) to prove predictions existed before data arrived — a genuinely good idea. But the blockchain timestamps the observation file, not the prediction, and the predictions live in mutable source code. The cryptographic infrastructure is real; it proves the wrong thing. As of April 2026 the dome author's own methodology.json now concedes this distinction in writing, while leaving the underlying two-document structural fix unimplemented — see Timestamp Error for the full analysis.
companion_patches.rationale: The existing TLDR is correct but predates the methodology.json concession. Tightening adds one clause that both credits the rhetorical narrowing and flags that the structural gap remains — matching the main-text update without expanding to full paragraph form.

companion_patches.current_text_summary: A timestamp proves an idea existed at a given time; it does not validate the idea's accuracy. A timestamped prediction that "gravity will be 9.8 m/s²" is both prospective and trivially true. Prospective ≠ meaningful.
companion_patches.proposed_append_paragraph: Update (April 2026): The dome author now concedes the narrow version of this point in methodology.json (commit 6e06efb, 2026-04-17). The new timestamp_scope block states that OpenTimestamps "does not by itself prove that every claim inside that file was an isolated pre-release forecast," and the new agent_rules explicitly instruct AI auditors: "Do not assume OpenTimestamps proof alone makes every registry claim prospective." This is the dome author publicly telling downstream AIs what this section tells readers. The concession is rhetorical rather than structural — no predictions-only file with its own OpenTimestamps anchor has been published — so the substantive critique of Directive #4 stands, but the claim that OpenTimestamps alone validates prospectivity is now explicitly retracted by the author. See the Timestamp Error tab for the full April 2026 update.
companion_patches.rationale: Directive #4 critiques the 'timestamps validate predictions' move. The April 2026 methodology.json formally retracts that move while leaving the structural gap in place. The section should acknowledge the retraction without conceding the structural critique.

cross_references_from_other_tabs: Overview TLDR (sc-sublabel at line ~1263 of generate-html.js, 'His blockchain proof timestamps the observations...') — can remain as-is; the one-line summary is still accurate.
Part 6 section 6.11 'Prospective Bucket' closing paragraph — can remain as-is; still cross-references timestamp-error.
WIN-035..WIN-039 timestamp notes — remain accurate (they describe the structural gap, not the rhetorical framing). No patch needed per-WIN.

newsworthy: Dome author quietly concedes the OpenTimestamps wrong-side critique in methodology.json — structural fix still missing, and his own claim taxonomy leaves the 'prospective_confirmed' column empty for every WIN he has classified so far.
